metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `init_core` handles failures returned by `run_ensures()` — at minimum, require that each unit failure is logged at WARNING level with the unit id and error detail, so operators can observe convergence failures.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how init_core surfaces run_ensures() failures to the operator/user: failures are returned as data but what init_core does with them (log, raise, warn, silently drop) is unspecified, leaving new failure points (git config writes, git commit) unobservable.
findings.location: What / 'Wire init_core to call run_ensures()'; AC lines on run_ensures() return value

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the '5 steps' count with the actual re-init call list in `init_core` (lines 209-213), which includes `_commit_gitignore` as a 5th step not named in the plan — either add it to the migration scope or document why it is intentionally left outside `run_ensures()`.
findings.finding: The plan lists exactly '5 current init-time steps' to migrate, but the re-init path of `init_core` (lines 209-213) calls `_commit_gitignore` in addition to the 5 named steps. Omitting it from the migration means `init_core` after WS1 will call `run_ensures()` PLUS a residual hand-listed `_commit_gitignore`, contradicting the stated goal of 'init_core calls run_ensures() instead of the hand-listed steps'.
findings.location: What section — 'Migrate the 5 current init-time steps'; AC2 — 'init_core calls run_ensures() (no behavior regression at init)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `_commit_gitignore` (`.gitignore` commit / migration) is a 6th ensure unit to migrate or explicitly left as a manual call in `init_core`; update the step count and wiring description accordingly.
findings.finding: `_commit_gitignore` is a 6th init-time step that lives in both the re-init branch (line 213) and the fresh-init path (line 242) of `init_core`, yet the plan names only 5 steps to migrate. After WS1, `init_core` will either drop `_commit_gitignore` (regression: new trackers won't get a .gitignore commit) or retain it as an orphaned manual call alongside `run_ensures()`, leaving the wiring asymmetric and the '5 steps' count wrong.
findings.location: ## What — '5 current init-time steps' / AC: 'All 5 init-time steps migrated in; `init_core` calls `run_ensures()` (no behavior regression at init)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `_commit_gitignore` as a 6th ensure unit in the plan (or explicitly name it as out-of-scope with a rationale), and update the '5 steps' count to '6 steps' to prevent it from being silently dropped during the `init_core` re-wiring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the marker-write conflict: either (a) explicitly state that WS1 stubs the recording as a no-op and WS2 wires the real write, or (b) define an interim format WS1 can use (e.g. a JSON list of ids at a fixed path) and note WS2 may revise it.
findings.finding: The plan says run_ensures() in WS1 'records the set of cleanly-applied ids to the applied-set marker' but also says 'WS2 owns the marker format', while the Scope Out list names 'the write-path hint (WS2)' — creating a conflicting signal about whether WS1 writes to the marker at all and, if so, in what format. An executor cannot implement the recording step without a format decision or an explicit interim stub decision.
findings.location: ## What — 'records the set of cleanly-applied ids to the applied-set marker (WS2 owns the marker format)' and ## Scope — 'Out: the write-path hint (WS2)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to ## Scope: 'WS1 stubs run_ensures() marker recording as a no-op (returns results in-memory only); WS2 adds the durable write.' Then remove 'records the set of cleanly-applied ids to the applied-set marker' from ## What until WS2 lands.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between What ('records cleanly-applied ids to the marker') and Scope ('Out: WS2') by either: (a) explicitly stating WS1 writes a stub/placeholder marker with a format TBD by WS2, or (b) removing the marker-write from the What section and deferring it entirely to WS2, then adding an AC for whichever path is chosen.
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes `run_ensures()` writing to the applied-set marker ('records the set of cleanly-applied ids to the applied-set marker'), but the 'Scope' section places WS2 (which 'owns the marker format') entirely out-of-scope, and no AC covers or verifies the marker-write behavior. This creates a contradiction: the described deliverable is simultaneously promised and out-of-scope, leaving implementers uncertain whether to write the marker in WS1 or defer it entirely.
findings.location: What section ('records the set of cleanly-applied ids to the applied-set marker (WS2 owns the marker format)') vs Scope section ('Out: the write-path hint (WS2)') vs AC4
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, change 'records the set of cleanly-applied ids to the applied-set marker (WS2 owns the marker format)' to 'returns per-unit results to the caller; marker persistence is deferred to WS2.' Add no new AC (WS2 will own it). This makes Scope and What consistent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a provisional marker interface (e.g., 'WS1 writes a JSON array of id strings; WS2 may extend the schema') or explicitly state that WS1's marker write is a no-op stub to be completed in WS2, so the cross-workstream coupling is acknowledged and mitigated.
findings.finding: WS1's run_ensures() writes to the applied-set marker, but the marker's format is owned by WS2 — the cross-workstream interface is unspecified in WS1, so the implementation must either guess a format or leave a stub, creating a silent coupling risk between the two workstreams.
findings.location: What section: 'records the set of cleanly-applied ids to the applied-set marker (WS2 owns the marker format)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the WS1 plan: 'For WS1, run_ensures() writes a JSON array of successfully-applied ids (format: ["id1", "id2", …]) which WS2 may extend with metadata fields; WS1 must not write fields WS2 does not define.' This pins the interface without over-specifying WS2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) define a minimal stub marker format in WS1 (e.g., 'a JSON file with a top-level `applied` key holding a list of id strings') that WS2 is contractually required to extend rather than replace, or (b) move all marker writes to WS2 and have WS1's run_ensures() return results without writing the marker.
findings.finding: The applied-set marker write is described as a WS1 deliverable ('records the set of cleanly-applied ids to the applied-set marker') but its format is deferred to WS2 ('WS2 owns the marker format'), creating an instruction-locality gap: the write code in WS1 must make concrete format choices without a specification.
findings.location: What section ('records the set of cleanly-applied ids to the applied-set marker (WS2 owns the marker format)'); Scope section ('Out: the write-path hint (WS2)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `_commit_gitignore` (and `_commit_precommit` for fresh init) should be added as a 6th (and 7th) ensure unit or explicitly called out as intentionally excluded with rationale, since both produce git commits and the plan's 'only step producing a git commit' assertion is refuted by the live code.
findings.finding: The plan asserts gitattributes is 'the only step producing a git commit', but `_commit_gitignore` (init.py:355) also produces git commits and runs on BOTH the re-init path (line 213) AND the fresh-init path (line 242). `_commit_precommit` (line 461) similarly commits on fresh init. This false-exclusive claim means the AC for idempotency ('a second run_ensures() makes zero git commits') is under-specified: the gitattributes unit is NOT the only commit-producing step that needs tree-checking.
findings.location: What section — '(check-then-commit — the only step producing a git commit)'; AC5 — 'gitattributes unit checks the tree before committing (no empty/no-op commits)'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the 'What' section to either: (a) add `_commit_gitignore` as a 6th migrated unit with the same 'check-then-commit' guard noted for gitattributes, or (b) explicitly state why it is excluded (e.g., 'gitignore is skipped because it only applies on first-create'). Also remove the word 'only' from 'the only step producing a git commit' since `_commit_gitignore` also commits.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the plan how `_commit_precommit` and `_gen_local_files` are handled after the `init_core` re-wiring (remain as direct calls, migrate as additional ensure units, or explicitly out-of-scope with justification).
findings.finding: The fresh-init path of `init_core` calls `_commit_precommit` and `_gen_local_files` (lines 244, 246) in addition to the 5 named units. The plan does not say whether these steps remain as manual calls in `init_core` after `run_ensures()` is introduced, are silently dropped, or are also migrated. This ambiguity makes the 'no behavior regression at init' AC unverifiable as written.
findings.location: ## What — init wiring / AC: 'no behavior regression at init'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete return type of each ensure-unit callable (e.g., a typed `EnsureResult` with `changed: bool` and optional `error`) and the aggregate return type of `run_ensures()` (e.g., `dict[str, EnsureResult]` + `list[str]` failures) in the plan or in `ensures.py`'s public interface section.
findings.finding: The 'changed/ok' result vocabulary and 'failures' return type of run_ensures() are referenced throughout the plan and ACs but never structurally defined: no concrete type (enum, dataclass, dict schema, namedtuple) is specified for what a unit callable returns or what run_ensures() aggregates.
findings.location: What section ('check-then-act callable that … reports changed/ok'); AC line 1 ('reporting changed/ok'); AC line 4 ('returns per-unit changed/ok + failures')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection to 'What' specifying: each unit callable returns an `EnsureResult(changed: bool, error: Exception | None = None)`; `run_ensures()` returns `tuple[dict[str, EnsureResult], list[str]]` where the second element is the list of unit ids that raised.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or a 'Failure contract' subsection) specifying run_ensures() partial-failure behavior — e.g. 'if a unit raises, it is recorded as a failure and remaining units still execute' — and require a test that verifies this.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `run_ensures()` returns 'per-unit results + failures' but no AC addresses the partial-failure contract: what happens when one unit raises mid-run (skip-and-continue vs abort), so the failure-path behavior of this new logic is unspecified and untested.
findings.location: ## What — 'returns per-unit results + failures'; AC4 — 'returns per-unit changed/ok + failures'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'If an individual unit raises an exception, run_ensures() catches it, records it as a failure for that unit, and continues running the remaining units; the final return includes the exception detail.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the per-unit exception fallback protocol in the plan: e.g., 'run_ensures() wraps each unit call in try/except, records the exception as a failure, and always continues to the next unit — never propagates mid-run.'
findings.finding: The fallback behavior when an individual ensure unit raises an unexpected exception inside run_ensures() is never specified: the plan says run_ensures() 'returns per-unit results + failures' but does not define the protocol (continue-and-collect, abort-all, log-and-skip, re-raise).
findings.location: What section ('returns per-unit results + failures'); AC line 4 ('returns per-unit changed/ok + failures')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'What': 'Each unit is invoked inside a try/except; any exception is caught, recorded as a failure for that unit id, and run_ensures() continues to the next unit unconditionally. The caller receives all failures in the return value and decides whether to surface them.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concurrency-safety mechanism (e.g., a per-repo advisory lock or git-level lock) for `run_ensures()` so concurrent invocations cannot both pass the check phase and both execute the act phase, particularly for the gitattributes git-commit unit.
findings.finding: The check-then-act pattern in run_ensures() has no concurrency safety specification: two concurrent init_core calls (or concurrent ensure runs from different entry points) can both pass the 'check' phase and both execute the 'act' phase, producing duplicate git commits on the shared tickets branch (gitattributes unit) or duplicate config mutations.
findings.location: What / run_ensures() design; AC: 'gitattributes unit checks the tree before committing'
findings.suggested_fix: Acquire a file-based advisory lock (e.g., `fcntl.flock` on a `.rebar-ensure.lock` file in the store) at the start of `run_ensures()` and release it after all units complete. Document the lock file path in the plan so WS2/WS3 wiring respects it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `run_ensures()` acquires the existing write lock (or init-lock) before executing commit-producing units, or require each commit-producing unit to be individually serialized — the current plan's check-then-act for gitattributes/gitignore is a TOCTOU race when two callers concurrently reach the re-init path of `init_core`.
findings.finding: The check-then-act mechanism for commit-producing ensure units (gitattributes, gitignore) has an unaddressed TOCTOU race: the plan specifies `run_ensures()` runs all units unconditionally but never specifies whether a lock serializes concurrent executions. Two concurrent `init_core` calls on an already-initialized store will both pass each unit's check phase and both attempt git commits, because the existing init-lock only guards the new-init path (lines 232-255 of init.py), not the already-initialized re-init path (lines 183-215) where `run_ensures()` will be called.
findings.location: What section ('run_ensures() runs ALL units unconditionally'); AC3 ('a second run_ensures() on a converged store makes zero changes and zero git commits'); AC5 ('gitattributes unit checks the tree before committing')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the `run_ensures()` spec: commit-producing units (gitattributes, gitignore) must run under the store's write lock (`_store/lock.py:write_lock`). Since `init_core`'s already-initialized re-init path currently holds no lock, the plan should either (a) acquire the write lock around `run_ensures()` in the re-init path, or (b) document that the idempotency guarantee for commit-producing units is best-effort under concurrent init (acceptable if init is rare), explicitly bounding the race.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC2 to explicitly verify removal: e.g., 'All 5 init-time steps migrated in; `init_core` calls `run_ensures()` replacing (not alongside) the previous hand-listed `_migrate_*`/`_ensure_*` calls.'
findings.finding: AC2 verifies replacement ('`init_core` calls `run_ensures()`') but does not explicitly verify removal of the old hand-listed steps from `init_core`. For a migration, both removal and replacement should be verifiable; without an explicit removal check, the old steps could remain alongside the new call, causing double-execution of side-effecting init logic.
findings.location: AC2: 'All 5 init-time steps migrated in; `init_core` calls `run_ensures()` (no behavior regression at init)'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: '…and the previous direct calls to `_migrate_gc_config`, `_ensure_merge_driver`, `_ensure_gitattributes`, `_ensure_scratch_exclude`, and `_ensure_env_id_seed` (or equivalent) are removed from `init_core`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC (e.g. for AC3: `python -c "from rebar._store.ensures import run_ensures; r1=run_ensures(repo); r2=run_ensures(repo); assert all(v=='ok' for v in r2.values())"` and `git -C <repo> log --oneline -1` showing no new commit after the second call; for AC2: name the existing test suite invocation that covers init_core).
findings.finding: No concrete proving command is specified for any acceptance criterion; AC2's 'no behavior regression at init' and AC3's 'zero changes and zero git commits' are stated as properties but the plan provides no test invocation, git-log check, or assertion that would produce evidence of success.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — all five ACs

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'no behavior regression at init' with a specific observable outcome, e.g. 'existing init_core integration tests pass unchanged AND a fresh init followed by run_ensures() produces the same repo state (same gitattributes content, same git config keys) as the previous hand-listed implementation.'
findings.finding: AC2's 'no behavior regression at init' is a subjective/effort-framed qualifier; it does not state a specific observable outcome (e.g. 'existing init_core tests pass' or 'git log shows the same commits as before migration') and cannot be evaluated deterministically in-session.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'All 5 init-time steps migrated in; init_core calls run_ensures() (no behavior regression at init)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out plan for the `init_core` rewiring: either document the single-commit revert procedure, or use an expand-contract sequence (keep old calls temporarily, validate run_ensures() parity, then remove old calls in a follow-up).
findings.finding: The plan rewires `init_core` (an existing entry point consumers rely on) to call `run_ensures()` instead of its current hand-listed steps, but provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequencing. T4 requires that a change consumers rely on be accompanied by an explicit rollback/back-out plan, not merely an acknowledgement that regression is undesired ('no behavior regression at init').
findings.location: What section ('Wire `init_core` to call `run_ensures()` instead of the hand-listed steps') and AC2 ('no behavior regression at init')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at minimum a module-level docstring in `ensures.py` (or a CLAUDE.md/ADR entry) specifying the ensure-unit contract (id conventions, callable signature, changed/ok semantics) so the pattern is discoverable before WS4 docs land.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new extensible architectural pattern (ensure-registry with a first-class ensure-unit contract: stable id + check-then-act callable + changed/ok semantics) but explicitly defers all documentation to WS4, leaving WS1 shipping an undocumented extensibility contract with no ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or inline design note.
findings.location: Scope section ('Out: … docs (WS4)') and What section (ensure-unit contract definition)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `DESIGN.md` stub or a CLAUDE.md paragraph in WS1 capturing: (1) ensure-unit shape (id: str, callable: (store) -> EnsureResult), (2) idempotency contract, (3) changed/ok semantics, (4) that run_ensures() is unconditional. This can be a 10-line note; full narrative docs stay in WS4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (or explicitly state) that each of the 5 migrated helpers has no callers other than `init_core`, OR add ACs for any secondary callers that must be updated.
findings.finding: The plan wires `init_core` to call `run_ensures()` instead of hand-listed steps, but does not confirm whether any of the 5 migrated `_migrate_*`/`_ensure_*` helpers are called from OTHER call sites beyond `init_core`; if they are, those callers silently lose their behavior after migration.
findings.location: ## What — 'Wire init_core to call run_ensures()'; AC2 — 'no behavior regression at init'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line assertion in the plan: 'Each migrated helper is called only from `init_core`; no other callers exist.' If secondary callers exist, list them in Scope and add an AC for their migration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC requiring at least one end-to-end test against a real (non-mocked) git store fixture: run `init_core` twice on a fresh repo and assert the second run produces zero git commits and zero changed units.
findings.finding: `run_ensures()` is wired as the default cutover path for `init_core`, but no AC requires a live/end-to-end test against a real git store; all idempotency and no-regression ACs could be satisfied by offline/mocked tests that never exercise actual git operations (gc config write, gitattributes commit, etc.), leaving the live path unvalidated.
findings.location: AC2 ('no behavior regression at init'), AC3 ('second run_ensures() makes zero changes and zero git commits'), AC5 ('gitattributes unit checks the tree before committing')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'An integration test initialises a real (tmpdir) git store, calls `init_core` (or `run_ensures()`) twice, and asserts: (a) first run converges all 5 units; (b) second run reports changed=0 and produces zero git commits.' This directly exercises the live path being defaulted to.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm WS3 (fsck/server-startup wiring) is a committed follow-on work-stream (not hypothetical), and document that justification in the plan so the single-call-site abstraction satisfies the Rule-of-Three exception for committed multi-consumer designs.
findings.finding: `run_ensures()` will have exactly ONE production call-site (`init_core`) when WS1 ships; the second and third wiring points (fsck, server-startup) are explicitly deferred to WS3. By the Rule-of-Three, introducing a named registry + dispatcher abstraction for a single call-site is premature — the abstraction cost is paid now but the second use-site is a future work-stream, not a current done-definition.
findings.location: What section / Scope section ('fsck/server-startup wiring (WS3)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Why or Scope section: 'WS3 (fsck + server-startup wiring) is a committed follow-on that will add ≥2 more `run_ensures()` call-sites; the registry is introduced now to avoid a second refactor.' This affirmatively documents the justification so reviewers and future readers don't mistake it for YAGNI.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
